title: Lease vs licence - a distinction that changes everything legally
p1:
Two people can occupy the same property, pay the same money, and still have completely different legal rights.

The difference may come down to one word: lease or licence.
p2:
Many assume a licence is just a shorter lease. That is not true. A lease usually gives a person a legal interest in the property, often with stronger protection. A licence generally only gives permission to use the space without creating that same ownership-like right.

The surprising part? Calling an agreement a “licence” does not automatically make it one. Courts often look at the actual arrangement, not just the label on the document.
p3:
This distinction matters in real life. A business owner using a shop, an employee staying in company housing, or a tenant renting a home may have very different rights depending on the nature of the agreement.

A simple takeaway: never judge a property arrangement by its name alone. Read the terms, the control given, and the rights created.
conclusion: Have you ever signed a property agreement without knowing whether it was a lease or a licence?
